MC: What is preprocessing in the context of machine learning?

column3: Any transformation applied to raw data to prepare it for modeling before training begins
A learning step where the model adjusts its weights based on the input features
The process of evaluating a model on the test set after training
A method for selecting which features to include in the model

MC: MC
Why is preprocessing considered an unsupervised operation?

column3: It is only used with unsupervised learning algorithms like clustering
It only looks at the feature data X and does not use the target labels y
It does not require a train/test split
It learns from both X and y but does not make predictions

MC: MC
Why does feature scale affect kNN classification performance?

column3: kNN uses a weighted formula where larger features receive larger coefficients
kNN requires all features to be on the same scale to store training data correctly
kNN computes distances between data points, and features with larger ranges contribute more to those distances
kNN uses feature importance rankings that are skewed by large-scale features

column3: kNN will ignore nonflavanoid_phenols entirely and use only proline
kNN will weight proline and nonflavanoid_phenols equally because it uses all features
kNN will automatically normalize the features internally before computing distances
Proline will dominate distance calculations and nonflavanoid_phenols will contribute almost nothing

MC: MC
What is the fit-on-training rule when using a scaler?

column3: Fit the scaler only on the training data, then use those statistics to transform both the training and test sets
Fit the scaler on the full dataset before splitting, then transform each set separately
Fit the scaler on the test set and apply the same statistics to the training set
Fit and transform the training set, then fit and transform the test set independently

MC: MC
Why is it incorrect to fit a scaler on the full dataset before splitting into train and test sets?

column3: It causes the scaler to compute incorrect statistics because the dataset is too large
It leaks information about the test set into the training process, making accuracy estimates too optimistic
It prevents the scaler from learning the correct mean and standard deviation
It causes the training and test sets to end up on different scales

column3: In a separate file that must be saved manually
In the transformed training array X_train_scaled
In the scaler object itself, accessible via attributes like scaler.mean_ and scaler.var_
In the original X_train array, which is modified in place

Why do decision trees not require feature scaling?

column3: Decision trees split on one feature at a time, so the relative scale between features does not affect the splits
Decision trees internally normalize all features before learning splits
Decision trees do not compute distances, but they still require features to be on a similar scale
Decision trees use feature importance rankings that automatically correct for scale differences

The resulting values will not fall between 0 and 1
The scaler will produce different results for the training and test sets

Fit on training, fit on test, transform both sets using combined statistics

Create the scaler, transform training data, then fit and transform the test data
